text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic appendectomy on a challenging retrocecal, retroperitoneal appendicitis case, navigating the anatomical complexities with precision.
The surgeon uses a hook to dissect the lateral peritoneal adhesions, attempting to visualize the appendix obscured by these adhesions.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to perform blunt dissection of the retrocecal fascia, gradually mobilizing the tissue to reveal the appendix.
The surgeon performs further blunt dissection, grasping the appendix to facilitate its mobilization and separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the mesoappendix, which is the fold of tissue connecting the appendix to the ileum, during the laparoscopic appendectomy.
The surgeon performs further blunt dissection to mobilize the appendix, continuing to separate the surrounding tissue layers in the retrocecal and retroperitoneal area.
The surgeon cuts the lateral peritoneal reflection and uses a hook to further dissect the lateral peritoneal reflection, revealing more of the appendix.
The surgeon seals the mesoappendix using a monopolar device, then uses a hook to elevate and cut the mesoappendix, creating a visible window in the tissue.
The surgeon continues to separate the tip of the appendix from surrounding adhesions, using blunt dissection to carefully free the appendix from attached tissues.
The surgeon uses a hook to separate the remaining adhesions around the appendix, identifying and skeletalizing the proper base of the appendix, revealing its anatomical structure.
The surgeon ligates the base of the appendix using a surgical clip, followed by a second ligation with another clip. The appendix is then cut at the base.
